11. DHCP Unique Identifier (DUID):
{
    "11. DHCP Unique Identifier (DUID)": [
        {"Original": "Each DHCP client and server has a DUID.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "DHCP servers use DUIDs to identify clients for the selection of configuration parameters and in the association of IAs with clients.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "DHCP clients use DUIDs to identify a server in messages where a server needs to be identified.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "See Sections21.2 and 21.3 for details regarding the representation of a DUID in a DHCP message.", "Adjusted": "See Sections 21.2 and 21.3 for details regarding the representation of a DUID in a DHCP message."},
        {"Original": "Clients and servers MUST treat DUIDs as opaque values and MUST only compare DUIDs for equality.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Clients and servers SHOULD NOT in any other way interpret DUIDs.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Clients and servers MUST NOT restrict DUIDs to the types defined in this document, as additional DUID types may be defined in the future.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "It should be noted that an attempt to parse a DUID to obtain a client's link-layer address is unreliable, as there is no guarantee that the client is still using the same link-layer address as when it generated its DUID.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Also, such an attempt will be more and more unreliable as more clients adopt privacy measures such as those defined in [RFC7844].", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If this capability is required, it is recommended to rely on the Client Link-Layer Address option instead [RFC6939].", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The DUID is carried in an option because it may be variable in length and because it is not required in all DHCP messages.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The DUID is designed to be unique across all DHCP clients and servers, and stable for any specific client or server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "That is, the DUID used by a client or server SHOULD NOT change over time if at all possible; for example, a device's DUID should not change as a result of a change in the device's network hardware or changes to virtual interfaces (e.g., logical PPP (over Ethernet) interfaces that may come and go in Customer Premises Equipment routers).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client may change its DUID as specified in [RFC7844].", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The motivation for having more than one type of DUID is that the DUID must be globally unique and must also be easy to generate.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The sort of globally unique identifier that is easy to generate for any given device can differ quite widely.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Also, some devices may not contain any persistent storage.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Retaining a generated DUID in such a device is not possible, so the DUID scheme must accommodate such devices.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}
12. Identity Association:
{
    "12. Identity Association": [
        {"Original": "An Identity Association (IA) is a construct through which a server and a client can identify, group, and manage a set of related IPv6 addresses or delegated prefixes.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Each IA consists of an IAID and associated configuration information.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The IAID uniquely identifies the IA and MUST be chosen to be unique among the IAIDs for that IA type on the client (e.g., an IA_NA with an IAID of 0 and an IA_PD with an IAID of 0 are each considered unique).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The IAID is chosen by the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "For any given use of an IA by the client, the IAID for that IA MUST be consistent across restarts of the DHCP client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client may maintain consistency by either storing the IAID in non-volatile storage or using an algorithm that will consistently produce the same IAID as long as the configuration of the client has not changed.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "There may be no way for a client to maintain consistency of the IAIDs if it does not have non-volatile storage and the client's hardware configuration changes.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If the client uses only one IAID, it can use a well-known value, e.g., zero.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If the client wishes to obtain a distinctly new address or prefix and deprecate the existing one, the client sends a Release message to the server for the IAs using the original IAID.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client then creates a new IAID, to be used in future messages to obtain leases for the new IA.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}

        {"Original": ".", "Adjusted": ""},
        {"Original": "+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+-+ Figure 7: DUID-LL Format The choice of network interface can be completely arbitrary, as long as that interface provides a unique link-layer address and is permanently attached to the device on which the DUID-LL is being generated.", "Adjusted": "The choice of network interface can be completely arbitrary, as long as that interface provides a unique link-layer address and is permanently attached to the device on which the DUID-LL is being generated."},

4. Terminology -> 4.2. DHCP Terminology:
{
    "4. Terminology -> 4.2. DHCP Terminology": [
        {"Original": "Terminology specific to DHCP can be found below.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "appropriate to the link An address is \"appropriate to the link\" when the address is consistent with the DHCP server's knowledge of the network topology, prefix assignment, and address assignment policies.", "Adjusted": "An address is \"appropriate to the link\" when the address is consistent with the DHCP server's knowledge of the network topology, prefix assignment, and address assignment policies."},
        {"Original": "binding A binding (or client binding) is a group of server data records containing the information the server has about the addresses or delegated prefixes in an Identity Association (IA) or configuration information explicitly assigned to the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Configuration information that has been returned to a client through a policy, such as the information returned to all clients on the same link, does not require a binding.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A binding containing information about an IA is indexed by the tuple <DUID, IA-type, IAID> (where IA-type is the type of lease in the IA -- for example, temporary).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A binding containing configuration information for a client is indexed by <DUID>.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "See below for definitions of DUID, IA, and IAID.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "configuration parameter An element of the configuration information set on the server and delivered to the client using DHCP.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Such parameters may be used to carry information to be used by a node to configure its network subsystem and enable communication on a link or internetwork, for example.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "container option An option that encapsulates other options (for example, the IA_NA option (seeSection 21.4) may contain IA Address options (see Section 21.6)).", "Adjusted": "A container option is an option that encapsulates other options (for example, the IA_NA option (see Section 21.4) may contain IA Address options (see Section 21.6))."},
        {"Original": "delegating router The router that acts as a DHCP server and responds to requests for delegated prefixes.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "This document primarily uses the term \"DHCP server\" or \"server\" when discussing the \"delegating router\" functionality of prefix delegation (see Section 1).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "DHCP Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol for IPv6.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The terms \"DHCPv4\" and \"DHCPv6\" are used only in contexts where it is necessary to avoid ambiguity.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "DHCP client Also referred to as \"client\".", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A node that initiates requests on a link to obtain configuration parameters from one or more DHCP servers.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The node may act as a requesting router (see below) if it supports prefix delegation.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "DHCP domain A set of links managed by DHCP and operated by a single administrative entity.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "DHCP relay agent Also referred to as \"relay agent\".", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A node that acts as an intermediary to deliver DHCP messages between clients and servers.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "In certain configurations, there may be more than one relay agent between clients and servers, so a relay agent may send DHCP messages to another relay agent.", "Adjusted": "No change"},

        {"Original": "A node that responds to requests from clients.", "Adjusted": "No change"},

        {"Original": "Depending on its capabilities, if it supports prefix delegation it may also feature the functionality of a delegating router.", "Adjusted": "Depending on its capabilities, if the DHCP server supports prefix delegation it may also feature the functionality of a delegating router."},
        {"Original": "DUID A DHCP Unique Identifier for a DHCP participant.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Each DHCP client and server has exactly one DUID.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "See Section 11 for details of the ways in which a DUID may be constructed.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "encapsulated option A DHCP option that is usually only contained in another option.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "For example, the IA Address option is contained in IA_NA or IA_TA options (see Section 21.5).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "See [Section 9 of [RFC7227]](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7227#section-9) for a more complete definition.", "Adjusted": "No change"},

        {"Original": "lease A contract by which the server grants the use of an address or delegated prefix to the client for a specified period of time.", "Adjusted": "No change"},

        {"Original": "This document primarily uses the term \"DHCP client\" or \"client\" when discussing the \"requesting router\" functionality of prefix delegation (see Section 1).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "retransmission Another attempt to send the same DHCP message by a client or server, as a result of not receiving a valid response to the previously sent messages.", "Adjusted": "No change"},

        {"Original": "Most options are singletons.", "Adjusted": "No change"},

        {"Original": "T2 The time interval after which the client is expected to contact any available server to extend (rebind) the lifetimes of the addresses assigned (via IA_NA option(s)) and/or prefixes delegated (via IA_PD option(s)) to the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "T2 is expressed as an absolute value in messages (in seconds), is conveyed within IA containers (currently the IA_NA and IA_PD options), and is interpreted as a time interval since the packet's reception.", "Adjusted": "No change"},

        {"Original": "transaction ID An opaque value used to match responses with replies initiated by either a client or a server.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]

15. Reliability of Client-Initiated Mess:
{
    "15. Reliability of Client-Initiated Message Exchanges": [
        {"Original": "DHCP clients are responsible for reliable delivery of messages in the client-initiated message exchanges described inSection 18.", "Adjusted": "DHCP clients are responsible for reliable delivery of messages in the client-initiated message exchanges described in Section 18."},
        {"Original": "If a DHCP client fails to receive an expected response from a server, the client must retransmit its message according to the retransmission strategy described in this section.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Note that the procedure described in this section is slightly modified when used with the Solicit message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The modified procedure is described in Section 18.2.1.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client begins the message exchange by transmitting a message to the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The message exchange terminates when either (1) the client successfully receives the appropriate response or responses from a server or servers or (2) the message exchange is considered to have failed according to the retransmission mechanism described below.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST update an \"elapsed-time\" value within an Elapsed Time option (see Section 21.9) in the retransmitted message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "In some cases, the client may also need to modify values in IA Address options (see Section 21.6) or IA Prefix options (see Section 21.22) if a valid lifetime for any of the client's leases expires before retransmission.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Thus, whenever this document refers to a \"retransmission\" of a client's message, it refers to both modifying the original message and sending this new message instance to the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client retransmission behavior is controlled and described by the following variables: RT Retransmission timeout IRT Initial retransmission time MRC Maximum retransmission count MRT Maximum retransmission time MRD Maximum retransmission duration RAND Randomization factor Specific values for each of these parameters relevant to the various messages are given in the subsections of Section 18.2, using values defined in Table 1 in Section 7.6.", "Adjusted": "The client retransmission behavior is controlled and described by the following variables: RT (Retransmission timeout), IRT (Initial retransmission time), MRC (Maximum retransmission count), MRT (Maximum retransmission time), MRD (Maximum retransmission duration), and RAND (Randomization factor). Specific values for each of these parameters relevant to the various messages are given in the subsections of Section 18.2, using values defined in Table 1 in Section 7.6."},
        {"Original": "The algorithm for RAND is common across all message transmissions.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "With each message transmission or retransmission, the client sets RT according to the rules given below.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If RT expires before the message exchange terminates, the client recomputes RT and retransmits the message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Each of the computations of a new RT includes a randomization factor (RAND), which is a random number chosen with a uniform distribution between -0.1 and +0.1.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The randomization factor is included to minimize synchronization of messages transmitted by DHCP clients.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The algorithm for choosing a random number does not need to be cryptographically sound.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The algorithm SHOULD produce a different sequence of random numbers from each invocation of the DHCP client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "RT for the first message transmission is based on IRT: RT = IRT + RAND*IRT RT for each subsequent message transmission is based on the previous value of RT: RT = 2*RTprev + RAND*RTprev MRT specifies an upper bound on the value of RT (disregarding the randomization added by the use of RAND).", "Adjusted": "RT for the first message transmission is based on IRT: RT = IRT + RAND*IRT. RT for each subsequent message transmission is based on the previous value of RT: RT = 2*RTprev + RAND*RTprev. MRT specifies an upper bound on the value of RT (disregarding the randomization added by the use of RAND)."},
        {"Original": "If MRT has a value of 0, there is no upper limit on the value of RT.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Otherwise: if (RT > MRT) RT = MRT + RAND*MRT MRC specifies an upper bound on the number of times a client may retransmit a message.", "Adjusted": "Otherwise: if (RT > MRT) then RT = MRT + RAND*MRT. MRC specifies an upper bound on the number of times a client may retransmit a message."},
        {"Original": "Unless MRC is zero, the message exchange fails once the client has transmitted the message MRC times.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "MRD specifies an upper bound on the length of time a client may retransmit a message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Unless MRD is zero, the message exchange fails once MRD seconds have elapsed since the client first transmitted the message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If both MRC and MRD are non-zero, the message exchange fails whenever either of the conditions specified in the previous two paragraphs is met.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If both MRC and MRD are zero, the client continues to transmit the message until it receives a response.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A client is not expected to listen for a response during the entire RT period and may turn off listening capabilities after waiting at least the shorter of RT and MAX_WAIT_TIME due to power consumption saving or other reasons.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Of course, a client MUST listen for a Reconfigure if it has negotiated for its use with the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}
18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.2:
{
    "18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.2. Client Behavior": [
        {"Original": "A client uses the Solicit message to discover DHCP servers configured to assign leases or return other configuration parameters on the link to which the client is attached.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A client uses Request, Renew, Rebind, Release, and Decline messages during the normal lifecycle of addresses and delegated prefixes.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "When a client detects that it may have moved to a new link, it uses Confirm if it only has addresses and Rebind if it has delegated prefixes (and addresses).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "It uses Information-request messages when it needs configuration information but no addresses and no prefixes.", "Adjusted": "A client uses Information-request messages when it needs configuration information but no addresses and no prefixes."},
        {"Original": "When a client requests multiple IA option types or multiple instances of the same IA types in a Solicit, Request, Renew, or Rebind, it is possible that the available server(s) may only be configured to offer a subset of them.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "When possible, the client SHOULD use the best configuration available and continue to request the additional IAs in subsequent messages.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "This allows the client to maintain a single session and state machine.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "In practice, especially in the case of handling IA_NA and IA_PD requests [RFC7084], this situation should be rare or a result of a temporary operational error.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Thus, it is more likely that the client will get all configuration if it continues, in each subsequent configuration exchange, to request all the configuration information it is programmed to try to obtain, including any stateful configuration options for which no results were returned in previous message exchanges.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Upon receipt of a Reconfigure message from the server, a client responds with a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message as indicated by the Reconfigure Message option (see Section 21.19).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client SHOULD be suspicious of the Reconfigure message (they may be faked), and it MUST NOT abandon any resources it might have already obtained.", "Adjusted": "The client SHOULD be suspicious of the Reconfigure message (Reconfigure messages may be faked), and the client MUST NOT abandon any resources it might have already obtained."},
        {"Original": "The client SHOULD treat the Reconfigure message as if the T1 timer had expired.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client will expect the server to send IAs and/or other configuration information to the client in a Reply message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If the client has a source address of sufficient scope that can be used by the server as a return address and the client has received a Server Unicast option (see Section 21.12) from the server, the client SHOULD unicast any Request, Renew, Release, and Decline messages to the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Use of unicast may avoid delays due to the relaying of messages by relay agents, as well as avoid overhead on servers due to the delivery of client messages to multiple servers.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "However, requiring the client to relay all DHCP messages through a relay agent enables the inclusion of relay agent options in all messages sent by the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server should enable the use of unicast only when relay agent options will not be used.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}

{
    "18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.2. Client Behavior -> 18.2.2. Creation and Transmission of Request Messages": [
        {"Original": "The client uses a Request message to populate IAs with leases and obtain other configuration information.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client includes one or more IA options in the Request message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server then returns leases and other information about the IAs to the client in IA options in a Reply message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client sets the \"msg-type\" field to REQUEST.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client generates a transaction ID and inserts this value in the \"transaction-id\" field.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST include the identifier of the destination server in a Server Identifier option (see Section 21.3).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST include a Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) to identify itself to the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client adds any other appropriate options, including one or more IA options.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST include an Elapsed Time option (see Section 21.9) to indicate how long the client has been trying to complete the current DHCP message exchange.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST include an Option Request option (see Section 21.7) to request the SOL_MAX_RT option (see Section 21.24) and any other options the client is interested in receiving.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MAY additionally include instances of those options that are identified in the Option Request option, with data values as hints to the server about parameter values the client would like to have returned.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client includes a Reconfigure Accept option (see Section 21.20) if the client is willing to accept Reconfigure messages from the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client transmits the message according to Section 15, using the following parameters: IRT REQ_TIMEOUT MRT REQ_MAX_RT MRC REQ_MAX_RC MRD 0 If the message exchange fails, the client takes an action based on the client's local policy.", "Adjusted": "The client transmits the message according to Section 15, using the following parameters: IRT REQ_TIMEOUT, MRT REQ_MAX_RT, MRC REQ_MAX_RC, MRD 0. If the message exchange fails, the client takes an action based on the client's local policy."},
        {"Original": "Examples of actions the client might take include the following: \\- Select another server from a list of servers known to the client \\-- for example, servers that responded with an Advertise message.", "Adjusted": "Examples of actions the client might take include the following: Select another server from a list of servers known to the client, for example, servers that responded with an Advertise message."},
        {"Original": "\\- Initiate the server discovery process described in Section 18.", "Adjusted": "Initiate the server discovery process described in Section 18."},
        {"Original": "\\- Terminate the configuration process and report failure.", "Adjusted": "Terminate the configuration process and report failure."}
    ]
}

{
    "18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.2. Client Behavior -> 18.2.7. Creation and Transmission of Release Messages": [
        {"Original": "To release one or more leases, a client sends a Release message to the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client sets the \"msg-type\" field to RELEASE.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client generates a transaction ID and places this value in the \"transaction-id\" field.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client places the identifier of the server that allocated the lease(s) in a Server Identifier option (seeSection 21.3).", "Adjusted": "The client places the identifier of the server that allocated the lease(s) in a Server Identifier option (see Section 21.3)."},
        {"Original": "The client MUST include a Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) to identify itself to the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST include an Elapsed Time option (see Section 21.9) to indicate how long the client has been trying to complete the current DHCP message exchange.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client includes options containing the IAs for the leases it is releasing in the \"options\" field.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The leases to be released MUST be included in the IAs.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Any leases for the IAs the client wishes to continue to use MUST NOT be added to the IAs.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST stop using all of the leases being released before the client begins the Release message exchange process.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "For an address, this means the address MUST have been removed from the interface.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "For a delegated prefix, this means the prefix MUST have been advertised with a Preferred Lifetime and a Valid Lifetime of 0 in a Router Advertisement message as described in part (e) of [Section 5.5.3 of [RFC4862]](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4862#section-5.5.3); also see requirement L-13 in [Section 4.3 of [RFC7084]](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7084#section-4.3).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST NOT use any of the addresses it is releasing as the source address in the Release message or in any subsequently transmitted message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Because Release messages may be lost, the client should retransmit the Release if no Reply is received.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "However, there are scenarios where the client may not wish to wait for the normal retransmission timeout before giving up (e.g., on power down).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Implementations SHOULD retransmit one or more times but MAY choose to terminate the retransmission procedure early.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client transmits the message according to Section 15, using the following parameters: IRT REL_TIMEOUT MRT 0 MRC REL_MAX_RC MRD 0 If leases are released but the Reply from a DHCP server is lost, the client will retransmit the Release message, and the server may respond with a Reply indicating a status of NoBinding.", "Adjusted": "The client transmits the message according to Section 15, using the following parameters: IRT REL_TIMEOUT, MRT 0, MRC REL_MAX_RC, MRD 0. If leases are released but the Reply from a DHCP server is lost, the client will retransmit the Release message, and the server may respond with a Reply indicating a status of NoBinding."},
        {"Original": "Therefore, the client does not treat a Reply message with a status of NoBinding in a Release message exchange as if it indicates an error.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Note that if the client fails to release the lease, each lease assigned to the IA will be reclaimed by the server when the valid lifetime of that lease expires.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}

{
    "18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.2. Client Behavior -> 18.2.9. Receipt of Advertise Messages": [
        {"Original": "Upon receipt of one or more valid Advertise messages, the client selects one or more Advertise messages based upon the following criteria.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "\\- Those Advertise messages with the highest server preference value SHOULD be preferred over all other Advertise messages.", "Adjusted": "Those Advertise messages with the highest server preference value SHOULD be preferred over all other Advertise messages."},
        {"Original": "The client MAY choose a less preferred server if that server has a better set of advertised parameters, such as the available set of IAs, as well as the set of other configuration options advertised.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "\\- Within a group of Advertise messages with the same server preference value, a client MAY select those servers whose Advertise messages advertise information of interest to the client.", "Adjusted": "Within a group of Advertise messages with the same server preference value, a client MAY select those servers whose Advertise messages advertise information of interest to the client."},
        {"Original": "Once a client has selected Advertise message(s), the client will typically store information about each server, such as the server preference value, addresses advertised, when the advertisement was received, and so on.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "In practice, this means that the client will maintain independent per-IA state machines for each selected server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If the client needs to select an alternate server in the case that a chosen server does not respond, the client chooses the next server according to the criteria given above.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST process any SOL_MAX_RT option (seeSection 21.24) and INF_MAX_RT option (see Section 21.25) present in an Advertise message, even if the message contains a Status Code option (see Section 21.13) indicating a failure, and the Advertise message will be discarded by the client.", "Adjusted": "The client MUST process any SOL_MAX_RT option (see Section 21.24) and INF_MAX_RT option (see Section 21.25) present in an Advertise message, even if the message contains a Status Code option (see Section 21.13) indicating a failure, and the Advertise message will be discarded by the client."},
        {"Original": "A client SHOULD only update its SOL_MAX_RT and INF_MAX_RT values if all received Advertise messages that contained the corresponding option specified the same value; otherwise, it should use the default value (see Section 7.6).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST ignore any Advertise message that contains no addresses (IA Address options (see Section 21.6) encapsulated in IA_NA options (see Section 21.4) or IA_TA options (see Section 21.5)) and no delegated prefixes (IA Prefix options (see Section 21.22) encapsulated in IA_PD options (see Section 21.21)), with the exception that the client: \\- MUST process an included SOL_MAX_RT option and \\- MUST process an included INF_MAX_RT option.", "Adjusted": "The client MUST ignore any Advertise message that contains no addresses (IA Address options (see Section 21.6) encapsulated in IA_NA options (see Section 21.4) or IA_TA options (see Section 21.5)) and no delegated prefixes (IA Prefix options (see Section 21.22) encapsulated in IA_PD options (see Section 21.21)), with the exception that the client MUST process an included SOL_MAX_RT option and MUST process an included INF_MAX_RT option."},
        {"Original": "A client can record in an activity log or display to the user any associated status message(s).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client ignoring an Advertise message MUST NOT restart the Solicit retransmission timer.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}

{
    "18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.2. Client Behavior -> 18.2.11. Receipt of Reconfigure Messages": [
        {"Original": "A client receives Reconfigure messages sent to UDP port 546 on interfaces for which it has acquired configuration information through DHCP.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "These messages may be sent at any time.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Since the results of a reconfiguration event may affect application-layer programs, the client SHOULD log these events and MAY notify these programs of the change through an implementation-specific interface.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Upon receipt of a valid Reconfigure message, the client responds with a Renew message, a Rebind message, or an Information-request message as indicated by the Reconfigure Message option (seeSection 21.19).", "Adjusted": "Upon receipt of a valid Reconfigure message, the client responds with a Renew message, a Rebind message, or an Information-request message as indicated by the Reconfigure Message option (see Section 21.19)."},
        {"Original": "The client ignores the \"transaction-id\" field in the received Reconfigure message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "While the transaction is in progress, the client discards any Reconfigure messages it receives.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The Reconfigure message acts as a trigger that signals the client to complete a successful message exchange.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Once the client has received a Reconfigure, the client proceeds with the message exchange (retransmitting the Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message if necessary); the client MUST ignore any additional Reconfigure messages until the exchange is complete.", "Adjusted": "Once the client has received a Reconfigure message, the client proceeds with the message exchange (retransmitting the Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message if necessary); the client MUST ignore any additional Reconfigure messages until the exchange is complete."},
        {"Original": "Duplicate messages will be ignored because the client will begin the exchange after the receipt of the first Reconfigure.", "Adjusted": "Duplicate messages will be ignored because the client will begin the exchange after the receipt of the first Reconfigure message."},
        {"Original": "Retransmitted messages will either (1) trigger the exchange (if the first Reconfigure was not received by the client) or (2) be ignored.", "Adjusted": "Retransmitted messages will either (1) trigger the exchange (if the first Reconfigure message was not received by the client) or (2) be ignored."},
        {"Original": "The server MAY discontinue retransmission of Reconfigure messages to the client once the server receives the Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message from the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "It might be possible for a duplicate or retransmitted Reconfigure to be sufficiently delayed (and delivered out of order) that it arrives at the client after the exchange (initiated by the original Reconfigure) has been completed.", "Adjusted": "It might be possible for a duplicate or retransmitted Reconfigure message to be sufficiently delayed (and delivered out of order) that it arrives at the client after the exchange (initiated by the original Reconfigure message) has been completed."},
        {"Original": "In this case, the client would initiate a redundant exchange.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The likelihood of delayed and out-of-order delivery is small enough to be ignored.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The consequence of the redundant exchange is inefficiency rather than incorrect operation.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}
18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.3:
{
    "18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.3. Server Behavior -> 18.3.3. Receipt of Confirm Messages": [
        {"Original": "SeeSection 18.4 for details regarding the handling of Confirm messages received via unicast.", "Adjusted": "See Section 18.4 for details regarding the handling of Confirm messages received via unicast."},
        {"Original": "Unicast transmission of Confirm messages is not allowed, regardless of whether the Server Unicast option (see Section 21.12) is configured or not.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "When the server receives a Confirm message, the server determines whether the addresses in the Confirm message are appropriate for the link to which the client is attached.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If all of the addresses in the Confirm message pass this test, the server returns a status of Success.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If any of the addresses do not pass this test, the server returns a status of NotOnLink.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If the server is unable to perform this test (for example, the server does not have information about prefixes on the link to which the client is connected) or there were no addresses in any of the IAs sent by the client, the server MUST NOT send a Reply to the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server ignores the T1 and T2 fields in the IA options and the preferred-lifetime and valid-lifetime fields in the IA Address options (see Section 21.6).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server constructs a Reply message by setting the \"msg-type\" field to REPLY and copying the transaction ID from the Confirm message into the \"transaction-id\" field.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server MUST include in the Reply message a Server Identifier option (see Section 21.3) containing the server's DUID and the Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) from the Confirm message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server includes a Status Code option (see Section 21.13) indicating the status of the Confirm message.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}

{
    "18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges -> 18.3. Server Behavior -> 18.3.1. Receipt of Solicit Messages": [
        {"Original": "SeeSection 18.4 for details regarding the handling of Solicit messages received via unicast.", "Adjusted": "See Section 18.4 for details regarding the handling of Solicit messages received via unicast."},
        {"Original": "Unicast transmission of Solicit messages is not allowed, regardless of whether the Server Unicast option (see Section 21.12) is configured or not.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server determines the information about the client and its location as described in Section 13 and checks its administrative policy about responding to the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If the server is not permitted to respond to the client, the server discards the Solicit message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "For example, if the administrative policy for the server is that it may only respond to a client that is willing to accept a Reconfigure message, if the client does not include a Reconfigure Accept option (see Section 21.20) in the Solicit message, the server discards the Solicit message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If (1) the server is permitted to respond to the client, (2) the client has not included a Rapid Commit option (see Section 21.14) in the Solicit message, or (3) the server has not been configured to respond with committed assignments of leases and other resources, the server sends an Advertise message to the client as described in Section 18.3.9.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If the client has included a Rapid Commit option in the Solicit message and the server has been configured to respond with committed assignments of leases and other resources, the server responds to the Solicit with a Reply message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server produces the Reply message as though it had received a Request message as described in Section 18.3.2.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server transmits the Reply message as described in Section 18.3.10.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server MUST commit the assignment of any addresses and delegated prefixes or other configuration information before sending a Reply message to a client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "In this case, the server includes a Rapid Commit option in the Reply message to indicate that the Reply is in response to a Solicit message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "DISCUSSION: When using the Solicit/Reply message exchange, the server commits the assignment of any leases before sending the Reply message.", "Adjusted": "When using the Solicit/Reply message exchange, the server commits the assignment of any leases before sending the Reply message."},
        {"Original": "The client can assume that it has been assigned the leases in the Reply message and does not need to send a Request message for those leases.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Typically, servers that are configured to use the Solicit/Reply message exchange will be deployed so that only one server will respond to a Solicit message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If more than one server responds, the client will only use the leases from one of the servers, while the leases from the other servers will be committed to the client but not used by the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}

{
    "18.3. Server Behavior": [
        {"Original": "For this discussion, the server is assumed to have been configured in an implementation-specific manner with configurations of interest to clients.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A server sends an Advertise message in response to each valid Solicit message it receives to announce the availability of the server to the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "In most cases, the server will send a Reply in response to Request, Confirm, Renew, Rebind, Decline, Release, and Information-request messages sent by a client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server will also send a Reply in response to a Solicit with a Rapid Commit option (seeSection 21.14) when the server is configured to respond with committed lease assignments.", "Adjusted": "The server will also send a Reply in response to a Solicit message with a Rapid Commit option (see Section 21.14) when the server is configured to respond with committed lease assignments."},
        {"Original": "These Advertise and Reply messages MUST always contain the Server Identifier option (see Section 21.3) containing the server's DUID and the Client Identifier option (see Section 21.2) from the client message if one was present.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "In most response messages, the server includes options containing configuration information for the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server must be aware of the recommendations on packet sizes and the use of fragmentation as discussed in [Section 5 of [RFC8200]](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8200#section-5).", "Adjusted": "The server must be aware of the recommendations on packet sizes and the use of fragmentation as discussed in Section 5 of RFC8200."},
        {"Original": "If the client included an Option Request option (see Section 21.7) in its message, the server includes options in the response message containing configuration parameters for all of the options identified in the Option Request option that the server has been configured to return to the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server MAY return additional options to the client if it has been configured to do so.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Any message sent from a client may arrive at the server encapsulated in one or more Relay-forward messages.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server MUST use the received message to construct the proper Relay-reply message to allow the response to the received message to be relayed through the same relay agents (in reverse order) as the original client message; see Section 19.3 for more details.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server may also need to record this information with each client in case it is needed to send a Reconfigure message at a later time, unless the server has been configured with addresses that can be used to send Reconfigure messages directly to the client (see Section 18.3.11).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Note that servers that support leasequery [RFC5007] also need to record this information.", "Adjusted": "Note that servers that support leasequery (RFC5007) also need to record this information."},
        {"Original": "By sending Reconfigure messages, the server MAY initiate a configuration exchange to cause DHCP clients to obtain new addresses, prefixes, and other configuration information.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "For example, an administrator may use a server-initiated configuration exchange when links in the DHCP domain are to be renumbered or when other configuration options are updated, perhaps because servers are moved, added, or removed.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "When a client receives a Reconfigure message from the server, the client initiates sending a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message as indicated by msg-type in the Reconfigure Message option (see Section 21.19).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server sends IAs and/or other configuration information to the client in a Reply message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The server MAY include options containing the IAs and new values for other configuration parameters in the Reply message, even if those IAs and parameters were not requested in the client's message.", "Adjusted": "No change"}
    ]
}
18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges:
{
    "18. DHCP Configuration Exchanges": [
        {"Original": "A client initiates a message exchange with a server or servers to acquire or update configuration information of interest.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A client has many reasons to initiate the configuration exchange.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Some of the more common ones are: 1\\.", "Adjusted": "Some of the more common reasons are:"},
        {"Original": "as part of the operating system configuration/bootstrap process, 2\\.", "Adjusted": "1. As part of the operating system configuration/bootstrap process,"},
        {"Original": "when requested to do so by the application layer (through an operating-system-specific API), 3\\.", "Adjusted": "2. When requested to do so by the application layer (through an operating-system-specific API),"},
        {"Original": "when a Router Advertisement indicates that DHCPv6 is available for address configuration (see[Section 4.2 of [RFC4861]](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4861#section-4.2)), 4\\.", "Adjusted": "3. When a Router Advertisement indicates that DHCPv6 is available for address configuration (see Section 4.2 of RFC4861),"},
        {"Original": "as required to extend the lifetime of address(es) and/or delegated prefix(es), using Renew and Rebind messages, or 5\\.", "Adjusted": "4. As required to extend the lifetime of address(es) and/or delegated prefix(es), using Renew and Rebind messages, or"},
        {"Original": "upon the receipt of a Reconfigure message, when requested to do so by a server.", "Adjusted": "5. Upon the receipt of a Reconfigure message, when requested to do so by a server."},
        {"Original": "The client is responsible for creating IAs and requesting that a server assign addresses and/or delegated prefixes to the IAs.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client first creates the IAs and assigns IAIDs to them.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client then transmits a Solicit message containing the IA options describing the IAs.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client MUST NOT be using any of the addresses or delegated prefixes for which it tries to obtain the bindings by sending the Solicit message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "In particular, if the client had some valid bindings and has chosen to start the server discovery process to obtain the same bindings from a different server, the client MUST stop using the addresses and delegated prefixes for the bindings that it had obtained from the previous server (see Section 18.2.7 for more details on what \"stop using\" means in this context) and that it is now trying to obtain from a new server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A DHCP client that does not need to have a DHCP server assign IP addresses or delegated prefixes to it can obtain configuration information such as a list of available DNS servers [RFC3646\"\")] or NTP servers [RFC5908 Server Option for DHCPv6\"\")] through a single message and reply exchange with a DHCP server.", "Adjusted": "A DHCP client that does not need to have a DHCP server assign IP addresses or delegated prefixes to it can obtain configuration information such as a list of available DNS servers (RFC3646) or NTP servers (RFC5908 Server Option for DHCPv6) through a single message and reply exchange with a DHCP server."},
        {"Original": "To obtain configuration information, the client first sends an Information-request message (see Section 18.2.6) to the All_DHCP_Relay_Agents_and_Servers multicast address.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Servers respond with a Reply message containing the configuration information for the client (see Section 18.3.6).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "To request the assignment of one or more addresses or delegated prefixes, a client first locates a DHCP server and then requests the assignment of addresses/prefixes and other configuration information from the server.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "The client does this by sending the Solicit message (see Section 18.2.1) to the All_DHCP_Relay_Agents_and_Servers multicast address and collecting Advertise messages from the servers that respond to the client's message; the client then selects a server from which it wants to obtain configuration information.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "This process is referred to as server discovery.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "When the client has selected the server, it sends a Request message to that server as described in Section 18.2.2.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A client willing to perform the Solicit/Reply message exchange described in Section 18.2.1 includes a Rapid Commit option (see Section 21.14) in its Solicit message.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Servers that can assign addresses or delegated prefixes to the IAs respond to the client with an Advertise message or Reply message if the client included a Rapid Commit option and the server is configured to accept it.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "If the server responds with an Advertise message, the client initiates a configuration exchange as described in Section 18.2.2.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "A server may initiate a message exchange with a client by sending a Reconfigure message to cause the client to send a Renew, Rebind, or Information-request message to refresh its configuration information as soon as the Reconfigure message is received by the client.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "Figure 9 shows a timeline diagram of the messages exchanged between a client and two servers for the typical lifecycle of one or more leases.", "Adjusted": "No change"},
        {"Original": "This starts with the four-message Solicit/Advertise/ Request/Reply exchange to obtain the lease(s), followed by a two-message Renew/Reply exchange to extend the lifetime on the lease(s), and then ends with a two-message Release/Reply exchange to end the client's use of the lease(s).", "Adjusted": "No change"},
